lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Manufacturing complexity
Let's talk about manufacturing, because ADCs are some of the most difficult biologics to make at scale, and the manufacturing complexity is a real driver of cost and supply.
To make an ADC, you need to first manufacture the antibody.

That alone is one of the more complex bioprocesses in pharma — you culture mammalian cells, usually Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cells, in large bioreactors, harvest the secreted antibody, purify it through a series of chromatography steps, and characterize it for sequence, structure, and post-translational modifications.
This is a months-long process and requires specialized facilities.
A modern monoclonal antibody plant costs hundreds of millions of dollars to build.
You also need to manufacture the payload-linker construct as a small molecule.
This is done through traditional pharmaceutical chemistry — typically a multi-step organic synthesis, often involving complex stereochemistry and sensitive functional groups.

The payloads are highly potent, so the manufacturing facilities have to handle them with the strictest containment — operators wear protective gear, the equipment is closed, the air handling is specialized.
A facility that can handle ADC payloads is called a high-potency API facility and is significantly more expensive to build and operate than a standard small-molecule plant.
Then you have to combine them.

The conjugation step is performed in solution — typically in a specialized bioreactor — and involves mixing the antibody with the payload-linker construct under defined conditions of pH, temperature, time, and stoichiometry.
The chemistry has to be quenched at exactly the right point to achieve the target DAR.
Then the conjugate has to be purified to remove unconjugated antibody, free payload, and any aggregates that formed during the reaction.
The analytics required to characterize a finished ADC are extensive.
You have to measure the average DAR.
You have to measure the DAR distribution — how many antibodies have zero payloads, how many have one, how many have two, and so on.
You have to measure the conjugation site occupancy — which positions on the antibody are loaded and which aren't.
You have to characterize aggregates.
You have to verify the antibody hasn't been damaged by the conjugation chemistry.
You have to check for free payload contamination.
Each of these is a separate analytical method, often involving mass spectrometry, liquid chromatography, and various biological assays.
The release specifications for a finished ADC are some of the most complex in all of biologics.
Then there's the supply chain.
You need a reliable antibody supplier and a reliable payload-linker supplier, and they have to coordinate so that you have inventory of both at the right time to run the conjugation.
Many ADC programs have struggled with supply constraints, particularly during the COVID era when biologics manufacturing globally was strained.
T-DXd had supply issues for several years after launch, and Daiichi has been investing heavily in expanded manufacturing capacity.
The economics of ADC manufacturing are correspondingly high.
A typical ADC dose costs the manufacturer thousands of dollars to produce.
The retail price to patients and insurers can be tens of thousands of dollars per dose.
T-DXd costs in the neighborhood of fifteen thousand dollars per infusion, and patients typically receive treatment for many months.
The total cost per patient can easily exceed two hundred thousand dollars over the course of therapy.
Some of that cost is the manufacturing complexity.
Some of it is recovering the development investment — these drugs cost billions of dollars to develop.
Some of it is the price the market will bear for life-extending cancer therapy.
The cost is driving real conversations about access and value, and it's likely to remain a flashpoint as ADCs expand into more indications.
The manufacturing complexity is also one of the reasons new entrants in the ADC space struggle.
It's relatively easy to design an ADC on paper.
It's much harder to actually make one reliably at scale, characterize it, demonstrate batch-to-batch consistency, and pass FDA inspection.
Companies that have invested in ADC manufacturing infrastructure — Seagen (now Pfizer), Daiichi, Roche, ImmunoGen (now AbbVie) — have a significant moat.
New companies often partner with contract manufacturers who have the necessary capabilities, and capacity at those CMOs is a constraint.
This is why the field's industrial structure looks the way it does.
ADCs are not just smart molecules; they're capital-intensive industrial products.
The companies that win are the ones that combine smart molecular design with manufacturing scale.
